findings.title: Proposal questions are answerable immediately, enabling execution before the referenced governance vote ends

best_agent_match.title: Questions are answerable immediately, enabling execution before the real governance vote is over

findings.title: Expiring approvals can permanently strand multi-transaction proposals in a partially executed state

best_agent_match.title: Expiring approvals can leave a multi-transaction proposal permanently half-executed

findings.title: Minimum-bond enforcement checks the highest historical bond, not the winning answer's backing

best_agent_match.title: Minimum-bond protection is bypassable because it checks the highest historical bond, not the winning answer's bond

rejected_candidates.title: The oracle question hides the executable payload behind opaque hashes instead of human-verifiable call data
rejected_candidates.reason: The contract cryptographically commits to the exact transaction preimages via `txHashes`; an attacker cannot swap in different calldata at execution time without changing the committed hashes. This is more a governance/UX visibility concern than a concrete protocol bug in the module.

rejected_candidates.title: DelegateCall allows arbitrary code execution in DaoModule context
rejected_candidates.reason: Arbitrary proposal execution, including `DelegateCall`, is an intended capability of a Safe-style executor once governance/oracle approval is obtained. This is part of the module's trust model, not a distinct implementation flaw; the call also executes through the executor rather than granting new standalone authority to arbitrary callers.

rejected_candidates.title: No bounds check on txIndex allows out-of-bounds array access
rejected_candidates.reason: An invalid `txIndex` only reverts that specific caller's transaction. It does not block valid executions or create a persistent denial of service because honest users can still supply a correct index.

rejected_candidates.title: No validation that target address is non-zero
rejected_candidates.reason: The proposal payload is intentionally arbitrary and already cryptographically committed. Sending to `address(0)` would require an approved proposal and is a governance choice, not an unintended exploit path introduced by the module.

rejected_candidates.title: Arbitrary target address allows any contract interaction
rejected_candidates.reason: This is the core purpose of a governance execution module: execute arbitrary approved calls via the executor. Without a separate approval bypass, unrestricted targets are not a vulnerability by themselves.

rejected_candidates.title: Oracle dependency is single point of failure for security
rejected_candidates.reason: Reliance on Realitio/arbitration is an explicit trust assumption of the design, not a bug in this implementation.

rejected_candidates.title: No validation on value parameter in executeProposal
rejected_candidates.reason: If the executor lacks sufficient balance, the call simply reverts. That does not create a new exploit beyond governance approving an unexecutable transaction.

rejected_candidates.title: Minimum bond check uses <= instead of >=
rejected_candidates.reason: `minBond == 0 || minBond <= oracle.getBond(questionId)` is the correct comparison for enforcing a minimum threshold. Allowing `minimumBond` to be set to zero is an explicit configuration choice by the executor, not a coding error.
